title: High-fidelity neutral atom gates leveraging low-rank Hessian optimization
authors: Genyue Liu, Guillaume Bornet, Deniz Kurdak, Mingxuan Xiao, Chenyuan Li, Bichen Zhang, Jeff D. Thompson
abstract: Quantum optimal control can produce fast and robust multi-qubit gates, but experimentally calibrating the resulting high-dimensional waveforms remains challenging because direct searches over large parameter spaces converge slowly. Building on the low-rank structure of quantum-control landscapes, we develop and benchmark a Hessian-based calibration method for optimal-control gates. The method identifies the few waveform directions that affect fidelity to leading order, with the number of directions set by the accessible leakage and coherent error channels, and optimizes only within this principal space using closed-loop experimental feedback. We apply this approach to an amplitude-robust controlled-Z gate on metastable-state 171Yb nuclear-spin qubits. Experimentally, we verify the predicted Hessian-sensitive directions and demonstrate rapid convergence of the optimization protocol. The optimized gate reaches a raw fidelity of 0.9959(2), increasing to 0.99902(7) after postselection on no detected loss, and the performance is essentially unchanged under laser-power variations of up to 20%. We further show that the same fidelity Hessian directions can correct certain Hamiltonian parameter errors. These results establish low-rank Hessian optimization as an efficient and physically motivated calibration strategy for high-dimensional optimal-control gates, which is broadly applicable to many qubit types.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, efficient calibration technique for quantum gates by exploiting the low-rank structure of the gate fidelity Hessian. By restricting optimization to the principal subspace defined by this Hessian, the authors successfully demonstrate high-fidelity controlled-Z gates on neutral atoms. This method offers a general, robust framework for improving quantum gate performance in complex atomic platforms.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of calibrating high-dimensional waveforms required for optimal quantum control to achieve high-fidelity multi-qubit gates.
main_result: A low-rank Hessian optimization method is established as an efficient calibration strategy, achieving high gate fidelities (e.g., 0.99902) with demonstrated robustness to environmental variations.
method: The core method involves analyzing the fidelity Hessian of the gate error, exploiting its low-rank structure to restrict optimization to a small, physically relevant principal subspace.
model_or_system: The work uses neutral atom qubits, specifically $^{171}	ext{Yb}$ nuclear-spin qubits, to implement an amplitude-robust controlled-Z (CZ) gate. The dynamics are modeled using optimal control theory applied to a multi-level atomic system.
key_observables: Gate fidelity ($F$), raw and post-selected error rates ($\epsilon$), and the eigenvalues/eigenvectors of the fidelity Hessian.
important_parameters: The rank ($r$) of the Hessian, which is determined by the number of accessible leakage and coherent error channels; laser power variation (up to 20%).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a second-order expansion of the infidelity in the control parameter error ($\delta\vec{\Omega}^T H \delta\vec{\Omega}$), and the low-rank structure is assumed to capture the dominant error channels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the CZ gate setup, the eigenvalue spectrum showing the few nonzero eigenvalues, the decomposition of the error into parallel and perpendicular components, and comparisons of optimization strategies.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by analyzing the fidelity Hessian, applies it to the CZ gate, demonstrates the optimization protocol using experimental feedback, and verifies the robustness and error correction capabilities.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, physically motivated mathematical tool (Hessian analysis) to simplify the notoriously difficult problem of optimizing complex quantum control pulses, directly applicable to state-of-the-art neutral atom quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Symmetric CZ gates and low-rank Hessian optimization. (a) Minimal three-level system for a symmetric Rydberg-mediated CZ gate, in which a pulse couples the qubit state |1⟩to a Rydberg state |r⟩with complex Rabi frequency ⃗Ω≡Ω(t)eiϕ(t). (b) In the two-qubit basis, the |01⟩and |10⟩states couple to the Rydberg manifold with Rabi frequency ⃗Ω, while the |11⟩state couples to the symmetric state |W⟩= (|1r⟩+ |r1⟩)/ √

FIG. 2. Gate benchmarking with three-outcome measurement. (a) Photon-count distributions collected from two successive images used to measure the atoms in |0⟩and |1⟩, respectively. Dashed lines indicate the image- classification thresholds, and colors indicate the assigned out- comes |0⟩, |1⟩, and loss. (b) Randomized benchmarking of RF single-qubit gates. The raw fidelity is F = 0.99935(3) (green), which increases to Fps = 0.999979(9) (blue) after postselecting on no detected atom loss. In all panels, error bars indicate 1σ uncertainties.

FIG. 3. Verification of the low-rank nature of the fidelity Hessian. (a) Four-level model of the states involved in the Rydberg gate. (b) Numerically optimized gate waveforms for the amplitude-robust gate. (c) Rydberg population during the gate when starting from |00⟩, |01⟩, and |11⟩. The small Zeeman splitting between |r⟩and |r′⟩results in a significant Rydberg population when starting in |00⟩. (d) Comparison between measured (circles) and calculated (squares) sensitivities λi of the CZ gate fidelity to distortions along the Hessian eigenvectors. Inset: representative measurements used to extract the sensitivity, with colors matching the highlighted points in the main figure. The fidelity...

FIG. 5. Optimizing gates with Hamiltonian param- eter errors. (a) CZ gate fidelity versus reduction of the Rydberg Zeeman splitting before and after Hessian optimiza- tion. A single optimization cycle along the original Hessian eigenvectors eliminates most of the added error, with an ad- ditional cycle needed at the largest miscalibration. (b) Opti- mized laser intensity and phase profiles for the nominal case (red) and the reduced-field case (blue, 1 −∆′ r/∆r = 0.14). Error bars indicate 1σ uncertainties.
